turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That says -- you have at 58 Ga. Code.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: 58 Ga. Code, the provisions on nuisances, misdemeanor and the right of the landlord to forfeit --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I think they --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: --are on 58.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I think they made a mistake, they sent me 38 [Laughs], you said, 58.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: 58, title 58.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Title 58.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Title 58 and the provision on manner of abating nuisances are in Title 72.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. Erdahl, you don't seem to argue -- I hadn't -- I just flipped your -- the pages of the brief and I therefore, do not know what's in it. But you don't seem to argue on the basis of the case within this Court that the question of search is a question of the unreasonableness under -- of the search. And therefore, I should think as one is not sympathetic with those cases, but taking them as the decisions of this Court, I don't see you're arguing on -- argue that it could hardly redeem the non-reasonable search if a policeman thought that the landlord authorized him to enter.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well, I -- I haven't had a chance to get that out yet --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: -- Mr. Justice.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is that argued in your brief?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well, yes -- yes --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Where?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: -- in this way that --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Where was it?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: -- under all the circumstances, this was a reasonable search.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I know, but I mean to be more specific.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: On the basis of the rights of the landlord?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm suggesting a line of argument with which, as I've said, I'm not sympathetic but might respect, if we're going to take the starting point of the decisions today.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well, on the matter of reasonableness and of course, this is the -- this is the -- of the state officers and of course, this is the test under Elkins. Bridgaman, the landlord took them to his house, he helped them through the window, he knew what was in there and obviously, he wanted to put a stop to it. I think it's fair to say that he was interested in repossessing his house and it seemed to it that it was to be -- the wasted any further.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You say he knew what was in there --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: He said he did.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- did he know any -- did he know anymore than the officers themselves knew?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: No. He said, "I know of what was in there by smelling it" and they smelled the same thing.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, in the case you argued, Mr. Erdahl, Johnson --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- sometime ago.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: This Court had that question that -- do you think that in -- in connection with the smell of opium?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And it was held that odor could be enough to make a search.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: On a warrant.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- if you got a search warrant --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: On a warrant.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- than you should present that to the judge or the magistrate, he might give a search warrant. So that so far as this case is concerned, it's against you is it not, unless you do depend on the right of the landlord --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- to do what was done.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Right. And that is precisely the basis on which we do defend it, Your Honor. I have said twice that if this had been a junket by these officers as in the Johnson case or as in the McDonald case, on their own without more, we wouldn't be here.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, do you think the law of Georgia is what controlled as to whether the landlord had a right --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well, I think --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- to do this?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: I think the law of Georgia is very important here --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- well, that's just been shown --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: -- because part of the picture of the reasonableness of what was done. Now, we -- we cannot assume on this record that these officers would have gone --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, I assume that there's a valid law of Georgia, which authorizes a landlord to do that under these circumstances and that -- that settled the question of reasonableness, isn't it?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well, the -- the Georgia law --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: It might not settle it under the federal law. It might conceive of them, they might not, that's the reason I asked you because you're defending on Georgia law as to that right?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well, there are Georgia statutes which are relevant on this question of reasonableness which I had related. They're part of the total picture particularly as the nuisance provision and -- and particularly under the statute on -- on Bridgaman's rights as a landlord. He obviously thought that he had a right to go in as he did.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Would that make a difference?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: I think it's part of the picture of reasonableness and he -- and the officers obviously thought that he had a right to allow them to go in, to tell them to go in. That's -- that's clear on this record. And so as Mr. Justice Frankfurter points out, it boils down to whether this was reasonable action. And we say it was -- it was in principle and in substance, an entry by the landlord in the assertion of his rights.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That brings you back to where they had the right, doesn't he?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I would find it a little difficult --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Now, I find --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- it a little difficult to say it was reasonable if he -- or either one of them to do it, if it's against the law for him to do what he did.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well, I'm sure that -- that the landlord --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) -- in law, one state or even the state of the Federal Government.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: I'm sure that the landlord, Bridgaman in this situation, had he knocked at the door and hadn't been opened to him, which is inconceivable since all there was in the house was a still. He could've said, "Get out here. Move! You're evicted."

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Suppose he had said, "No, we won't get out." What could he do then?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well, he'd have to go to court.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: But his mouth would not be shut as to what he saw in there, the still.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But you're talking about he has the right to go there and evict them.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now, the Georgia law, as far as I can gather from you doesn't -- doesn't provide that that he has a right to go there and either summarily evict them or to summarily abate the -- the nuisance or to do anything else. He has certain rights of -- of forfeiture. He has certain rights of entry which he can --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- if he can't get peaceably, he can get through a --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: But --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- through a writ.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And he has maybe other rights too, he has a right to inform the police, but does he have the right to go and climb through a closed window of the bathroom in order to look into that house and search it to see if there is any violation of law. Now, where is your Georgia law for that? That's the basis (Voice Overlap ) --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: The Georgia law on that is the -- is the common law. There is no -- the -- I don't -- I am -- we have found nothing in the Georgia law giving a landlord the right to go through a window.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Have you found it in --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: But he --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- any of these statements?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: But he has the right that is centuries old to enter his property, to view waste to -- to collect rent, to make repairs, to stop a nuisance. That's one of the situations in which Tiffany says, "He has a right to entry -- enter." And certainly --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. Erdahl --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: -- there was a nuisance here, a very grievous nuisance.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Could you forgive me for saying so, I do not think you have yet, even what you've just said, argued on the basis of this Court's decisions regarding search and seizure. It is not a course of decision that every entry without a warrant is presumptively an unlawful entry.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is not the line of reasoning that this Court has taken.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: That was said even in Trupiano.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It has dealt with unreasonable search as the affirmative authorization. Unreasonable search isn't the exception to the demand for a warrant. Otherwise, the cases wouldn't be -- be what they've been that even if there was time to get a warrant, it doesn't matter. Therefore, the question isn't -- the question is whether in -- under all the circumstances, this was an unreasonable thing to do.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And if the landlord thought he had the right to do, I do not see and it is not a -- isn't on egregious assumption, it isn't defensible assumption. It's one that one has controversy about as to -- to the extent of a landlord's power. I don't see how he can argue it's unreasonable, because he might be wrong as to Georgia law.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Right. Well, you've taken the words out of my mouth, Mr. Justice.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I'll give them back to you. [Laughter]

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: And while we don't cover this in our brief, I think I ought to touch on it. Let us suppose -- let us suppose that Bridgaman exceeded his rights in view of the discussion here, I think I better touch on this.Let us suppose that Bridgaman exceeded his rights when he, not because he enters, he had the right to enter and he had the right to enter against the will of the tenant. But he exceeded his rights in the mode of entry, in the mode of entry. That at most, would be a technical trespass against the rights of this man, Chapman.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Is that where this window partly opened or --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: No, it doesn't appear that it was cracked at all, it was unlocked. All the other windows were locked.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: He assumed it was fully closed, but unlocked, is that it?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Right. And I assume further that the reason they could see it was unlocked was because the shade, you know, normally is on the inside of the locked window.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, would it make a difference if the window was opened because even open windows would (Inaudible) go in?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: No.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- when he cannot go in.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: No.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No part of invitation --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: And as that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- on a window that -- that you have to lift.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: And that -- that, I agree. And as I've said, this was in substance and in principle, an entry by Bridgaman. But let us assume that he overstepped his rights in the manner in which he exercised his rights. The cases in this Court had not allowed the admissibility of evidence to turn on such technical wrongs, civil wrongs. I refer to Burdeau against McDowell, the case in which McDowell's papers were taken out of his office by private persons without his authorization and the Court said that -- and they were turned over to the United States Attorney for prosecutions, for presentation to agree in jury. The Court said, "We're not going to stop to consider the wrong done to McDowell or what his rights or remedies maybe against the wrongdoer." In the McGuire case, the agents had a warrant, search warrant, for liquor and when they executed the warrant, they kept to court and destroyed all the rest and it was claimed that this made them trespasser ab initio, because the destruction, their act of destroying the liquor was illegal and the Court agreed to before it and oppressed it. But the Court said, "This doesn't affect the validity of the search for and the seizure of and retention of the Court be kept." They said that was valid, that was distinct from the civil wrong, the wrong done in destroying. And more recently, in On Lee, the Court reiterated that principle. It was their claim that you recall, that was the case of the informer who went in wired the sound, radio transmitter into On Lee's Laundry and engaged them in conversation, he was a friend of On Lee and On Lee made the incriminating statements, which were picked up on the radio and heard by an agent outside who had the receiving sense. And it was contended there that on -- the informer was either a -- a trespasser ab initio or he was a trespasser when he went in, because the entry was fraudulent as against On Lee. And the Court said there that the McGuire case controls the question and that what -- whether -- whether the informer's conduct was unlawful or not, this did not affect the Government's right to use the evidence. And I think that this case is an even stronger case for the application of that principle because there, the informer who was guilty of betraying his friend's confidence was acting under the auspices of agents who sent him in wired for sound. Here, the officers wrecking under the auspices of the landlord who had no ulterior motive, whatever.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Was that case a public place -- a public place of business?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Yes, it was.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: They walked in through an open door that the public had --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- had the right to go into and --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: That's true. But this was Bridgaman's house and he had rights of entry as a landlord. Now, I think we can test -- test the question this way, test the principle. Supposing -- let us suppose that Bridgaman in good faith, on his discovery, had resorted to self-help or -- or had gone and gotten some of his friends to help him and he and his friends went into the house the same way. There would be -- Chapman would have absolutely no right to complain of his discovery -- of Bridgaman's discovery of his Chapman's still, in Bridgaman's house. And Bridgaman would be free to -- to testify about it, testify as to what he saw at the still.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well under the case, that's --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: That's under --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- that's private -- that's true of any private individuals --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) the owners.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: That's right. Now --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: That had -- what you've just said doesn't depend on -- doesn't -- doesn't stand from the fact that Bridgaman was the owner of this house, but just that he was not an officer, isn't that true?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well, yes. It wouldn't make any difference here if had been a stranger to the house, to the lease. That's right.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: A burglar broken into the house --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Yes, a burglar, sure.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- he could've testified.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Sir, I agree -- I agree.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But you want us to decide it on that basis?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Let us further suppose that -- well, of course, it would've been foolhardy on Bridgaman's party to go in himself. He might've subjected himself to danger. The moonshiners are not very nice people. He might have been (Inaudible) So what is he -- what he did he do? He did the -- a person, he did -- he did the thing that any natural, normal, law abiding person would do. He went to get the police. He went to get the police to protect him and to aid him in the exercise of his rights. And when they got there, he, as I said before, helped boost Harbin on his shoulders and threw the window. Now, let's suppose --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: You might just --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: As a matter of fact, Bridgaman did testify to like effect as the officers, as to what he saw in the house. After Harbin got in, unlocked the door, Bridgaman went in and he said there, it was all piled up in the living room. That's all there were, that was in this house, that -- that still and those mash beds, but to get back. Let -- let's suppose that when they -- when they got to the house, the officers -- and Bridgaman says, "Go on in" and the officers emerged and said, "Well, I don't know of whether we can go in, but you can go in or maybe you can go in as landlord." And let's suppose, instead of Harbin being boosted through the window, Bridgaman had been boosted through the window. Now, I don't -- he then had -- it was his house, he had been boosted through. He could have then opened the door, called the officers in and really have the same situation has actually occurred here.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Would that have been a good search then?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: I think so.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: He might be just (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: I think so, the landlord going into his own house not withstanding that he was -- had the aid and the protection there of the -- of -- of the police and not withstanding that he and they may have had in mind to -- to seize a still and -- and catch a moonshiner. I think that makes no difference. Now, the short of it is, I don't think that this -- the validity of this entry and that's the beginning and the end of our problem. I don't think its validity should turn on who went in the window first. Now, Mr. -- finally, Mr. Elliott said that, I adverted to this -- the decision which we asked for here, putting the -- putting tenants to the -- at the mercy of landlords. Well, that is not so at all and we had an answer to such a suggestion, a very short time ago in the Abel case. The -- I think we can depend upon the federal courts and upon this Court to be alert, to prevent any abuse of the rights of landlords to enter and to take in -- enter their premises and to take in police with them. If there's collusion to circumvent constitutional restrictions on law enforcement, then it will be time to call a halt -- halt and those matters, if they should arise, should -- will depend upon the evidence and the concrete findings in such a situation.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Isn't that case turned on the consent of the -- of Abel to -- to let the owner in?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: The consent of Abel to --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: -- let this owner.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: To let the owner in?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: I don't understand.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, I thought in Abel, the -- the Court said that -- that he had consented to them coming there and taking those let material out of the waste basket.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Oh, yes sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Oh well --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Two segments to Abel. The -- the wastebasket part and the earlier part which occurred when he was arrested by the immigration officers.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, but when he was arrested by the immigration officers, that wasn't through the -- through the proprietor of the hotel.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Oh, no.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: No.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- they came there with administrative warrant.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: That's right. That's what --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- the other things -- the other things that involved the -- the landlord as I recall it, there were done according to the Court with his consent.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: In Abel?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: In Abel.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: I don't recall that the landlord, the hotel keeper, the innkeeper --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: The hotel.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: -- had anything to do with it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: The hotel, it was a hotel.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Had anything to do with it.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If they entered his room.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Oh, oh, yes. Yes. I would -- that's why -- yes, the wastebasket in part of the -- yes, but I mean on the entry. I don't think the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: The landlord --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: -- the hotelkeeper.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- didn't have anything to do with the entry. That was done by --

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- that was done by the administrative crew and the administrative warrant gotten by the -- gotten by the immigration authorities on the information of the FBI.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Yes. The FBI --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I don't know how to turn here?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: -- turned it over to --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I was outrageous.

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well, I -- I used Abel, Mr. Chief Justice, only in answer to Mr. Elliott's suggestion that the decision we asked for here would open wide the opportunities or allusion between landlords and police whereby, they could snoop and get evidence on tenants and landlords that walk in, take the police with them. I say that only for the proposition that it -- those cases should be taken cared of, if they should arise and on the basis of the particular evidence because after all, as Mr. Justice Frankfurter suggested a while ago, "In all these questions, you always come back to the question of reasonableness, in all the circumstances."

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Very generous of me to make that suggestion, wasn't it?

turns.name: ROBERT S. ERDAHL
turns.text: Well, I intended to get to it Mr. Justice [Laughs] --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't mean generous to you, I mean generous to the idea.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Elliott.

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: May it please the Court. I think we have discussed the number of aspects in all of the aspects of this case. I do however think, it is most important to consider that we must construe the rights of a tenant in this case, as opposed to the rights of a homeowner. And I do not think that because a man rents his house that he should use -- lose any of his constitutional rights such as under the Fourth Amendment to contest or to ask that certain evidence be suppressed, simply because, he is the tenant in the house, rather than the owner. I cannot -- that does not set well with me. I -- I'm wondering if this case is not reversed. If the house is owned by the United States Government, if the federal agents couldn't just go in on the same basis that the Government claims that Bridgaman can -- could go in under this case. And would it not be stripping away the individual rights of those tenants in the various housing projects throughout the country. I -- I think that it is -- would be too far reaching and would really be a question that this Court is not going to allow, the rights of individuals to be stripped away in that manner.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you. Have you cited any cases which showed -- tell the -- what is the Georgia law with reference to the right of a landlord to enter a house, if he finds that the law had been violated in it, in a way that may entail some kind of guilt and -- and put some kind of guilt on him?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: Alright. I have -- I'd like to refer to the two statutes and I think that's all there is under Georgia law that we've already referred.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: No cases of any kind?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: 58 Ga. Code 109.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I have that code.

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: It -- it says -- it reads as follows, "Common nuisance, places defined as the following are hereby declared to be common nuisances and maybe abated as such upon complaint of the Attorney General or the Solicitor General of the circuit or any citizen or citizens of the county." And then it goes on and refers to a room used for the manufacture of unlawful -- unlawful manufacture of whiskey.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: So that this was, if it was being used for that purpose, a nuisance under Georgia law.

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: Yes, in other words, a person, a landlord or any other person must go into court if--

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: -- they want to abate. They cannot break and enter either under the Georgia law as it --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And I think --

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: -- stand.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Suppose he had known that this was being used for that purpose and had not reported it, what would've been his liability under Georgia law for that nuisance?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: I think it's very possible that the Court would consider or in any action to takeaway his property, to condemn and takeaway his property, I think they could consider that factor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, I have before me, one of the cases cited. It says, "They have considered it and having reached the conclusion that he had knowledge of it, that they had refused to let his house be unpadlocked when it was padlocked as a nuisance under the statute. That's the -- under the 96 Ga. Code 802. Is -- is that your understanding of the law?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: That is, in that particular case, it seems --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: -- that the owner --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- is it your understanding of the law that --

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: It seems that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- that a guilt and knowledge of the use of his premises by landlord, use of his premises for manufacturing liquor, would subject his property to seizure by the State of Georgia, if he knew about it and -- and made no report of it.

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: I can say that it was subjected under the federal laws. I --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Under the federal rule.

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: -- I cannot reply under the state -- state law.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Have you then -- have either one of you cited the federal statute under which that would be the case?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: No, not -- not in our brief.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You say it would be forfeitable under federal law?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: Under federal law. It could be if it's used for illegal purposes, it could be forfeitable and it --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Was it not?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: And -- and in an instance, where a landlord fails to report something, he could be therefore, considered a conspirator to the extent of entering into the operation or harboring a criminal activity and therefore subject -- subjected to the penalties that would come to anyone who participated in any such action. I would also like to refer to the --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: May I ask you please sir, as he -- may I ask you please, sir? Has he discharged his full duty when he has made the report?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: Yes. I believe that in this particular case, what we have is this. Now -- let -- let me make one or two background comment. The Government says they could've entered to collect rent. Well, now I -- I don't know what that's got to do with this case. In -- in other words, a man -- a landlord goes to collect his rent and he knocks on the door and nobody's there, what is he going to do? Go into bathroom window and find the pocketbooks and collect his rent? I don't think that that's the case. I think it has no applicability here. And getting back to your question, Justice Whittaker, I will say this that I think that this man actually just as any other citizen when it came to his attention that -- that he thought the law was being violated there, he made a report not to the general law enforcement officers, but to the liquor agents. And when he made that report and told them, "If you come out here, I'll show where it still is," I feel that he had fulfilled his obligations. I feel that from that point on, it was within the hands of the officers and the officers should have known what to do and it -- the law clearly says what those officers should do in the abatement of an action.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, under your law, could they, the officers, derive any author -- entry authority from him, the landlord?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: Not -- not to the extent of breaking and entering. There are no provisions that I know of on the -- in the codes of Georgia, in the law of Georgia to allow it. Turning to the common law, there is no provision there to allow any such entry to the extent of breaking -- of breaking a window or a door. And by breaking the seal on this house, by opening a closed window is very clearly a breaking and it is not permitted by -- by federal -- by common law and there is no provision under state law for any such action that was -- as was done here. This was simply -- very clearly, a report to the officers by a citizen that I think the law is being violated and turning it over to the officers to take the proper action.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And you think it's -- and you would regard it as a -- a trespass for him if he's under this duty at least of reporting, to verifying his sense of smell by visual -- by visual proof?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: I think it would've been --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Do you think that would be trespass?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: I think it would've been trespass for him to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The door was open.

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: -- enter it, to do anymore than he had done before the officers came. And I think the officers had no more right to do more than he could have done.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Practically yes, but do you think that landlords can't take measures, as one, he could be certain --

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: There was --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- that they were mashing things up if -- what if he's under a duty to report?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: There was no compelling --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He knocked at the door and found that nobody is in.

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: There was no compelling circumstance here. There was no reason in the world why the officers could not obtain a warrant. There was full and ample opportunity.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is not the requirements of this Court as to this -- that is not the test of ambiguity.

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: The -- I have two or three other comments that I'll try to be very brief on. Even though property rights would have been lost, either under this Georgia statute that is quoted in the brief of the Government, I think that that would not strip away from his -- him his constitutional rights. I also asked the Court to please, if they consider the quoted Section 58 Ga. Code 106 that they also consider the Section 58 Ga. Code 101 which is referred in that Section, but is not quoted in the brief of the Government.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: What does that Section provide, Mr. Elliott?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: That Section provide, Your Honor, the term "prohibited liquors and beverages" and that is in quote, used in any law to promote temperance or to suppress the evils of intemperance shall include the following. One, alcohol, alcoholic liquors, spirituous liquors and all mixed liquors, any part of which is spirituous, foreign or domestic spirits or rectified or distill spirits; absent, whiskey brandy, rum and gin and then two, Venus liquors and beverages, three, "Nothing in this chapter shall apply to fermented beverages made from malt in hold or in thought or any similar beverages." I feel --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: What -- what's the -- to what effect do you quote that?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: Well, in this particular case, there was nothing in this house, but mash. There -- there was no liquor in this house. And I do not believe this particular Section has any applicability here because I do not think that was anything in that house that would exercise any forfeiture. And I also want to point out that the Section provides, shall at the option of the landlord, work a forfeiture. There's no indication that the landlord exercised any or -- or option to forfeit the rights of this tenant. There's no indication that he's -- took it upon himself to say, "There is a forfeiture and therefore, you are not required to pay anymore rent under any contract that may exist."

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What was the mash made of?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: I'm not sure that I can answer --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Does the record show?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: -- that question.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Does the record show?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: The record shows that -- that it was simply mash. I -- I can't help you on that, sir. I think it was a type of mash that has been left to ferment, as I understand it. There was no whiskey involved in this case at all, and none in the house. So, I don't think there's --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Does the record show what --

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: -- applicability of this statute.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- does the record show what it was to be if it was carried forth before a provision?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: I -- I'm sorry, I didn't understand your --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did the evidence show what is -- would have been, had the process could've been completed?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: As I understand, I -- I think perhaps it would've been white lightning if the process [Laughter] had been --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You mean corn liquor --

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: -- had been terminated.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You mean corn liquor?

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: That would be hard liquor. What they [Laughter] term as white whiskey or --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: White lightning. [Laughs]

turns.name: J. SEWELL ELLIOTT
turns.text: There is no -- there's nothing in the Georgia law to permit this landowner to do what he did. I further say that I do not agree with the contention of the Government that -- in other words, they present this argument. They say that the landlord actually went in and took the -- the agent with him. I don't interpret the facts and I don't think the facts support that conclusion. I think, at most, what it was, it was a report to the liquor agent that I think I know where you can find the still and he helped them locate it. And at that stage of the game, it was turned over to the officers and they should've known exactly what to do and that a warrant was required. Also, it was a case of going around the house. The Harbin, who is the officer who entered, went all the way around the house, tried all the windows until he found one that was unlocked and that one he went into. I also would like to point out that this house or the evidence clearly shows that the house was not back in the woods to such a -- to such an extent that it could not be clearly seen.I t was a little longer than the length of the courtroom in the -- in the trial court from the road. It could be clearly seen from the road. It was not hidden back in the woods as a still house might be. And I want to thank this Honorable Court for your indulgence in the presentation of this case. Thank you.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Gentlemen, as to the next case, I think it would serve no purpose to -- to start at this late -- late moment. We will start fresh on it Monday morning. We'll recess now.
